regulationSummary.overview: Indiana has no gold deposits and is not a gold prospecting destination. The state is underlain by Paleozoic limestone, shale, and sandstone formations beneath thick glacial drift. Indiana's mineral resources include limestone (world-class building stone from the Bedford/Bloomington area), coal, and gypsum — but no gold. The glacial gravels of the Wabash and White River drainages carry transported minerals from Canadian shield rocks, but no significant gold. This entry is included for completeness.
keyRules: Indiana does not have a recreational gold prospecting permit system — there are no productive gold deposits in the state.
Rock collecting on Hoosier National Forest land in southern Indiana is allowed for personal use in reasonable quantities.
In-stream work in Indiana waterways requires coordination with the Indiana Department of Environmental Management (IDEM).
regulationSummary.hpaInfo: Not applicable — there are no gold-bearing streams in Indiana.
regulationSummary.suctionDredgeStatus: Not applicable — Indiana has no productive placer gold streams.
regulationSummary.handPanning: Gold panning in Indiana streams is not a viable activity. The state has no gold-bearing geology. Southern Indiana's Hoosier National Forest is excellent for geode collecting (especially near the town of Medora) but not gold prospecting.
regulationSummary.disclaimer: Regulations change. Verify current rules at the official sources listed below before going out.

officialResources.title: Indiana Geological and Water Survey
officialResources.description: State geological survey — Indiana has no gold-bearing geology; the survey covers limestone, coal, and industrial minerals.

officialResources.title: Hoosier National Forest
officialResources.description: USFS-managed land in southern Indiana — allows recreational collecting. Known for geodes and limestone fossils, not gold.
